hand.title: Daily Hand

events.text: Your cards — 4, A. You raised aggressively before the flop, putting the pressure on your opponent, who calmly called to see the next cards.
Flop — 10, Jack, 5. You bet to test the waters, and your opponent called, choosing to stay patient and see how the hand developed.
Turn — King. You bet again, but this time your opponent raised, indicating they had just made a significant connection with the board.
River — 3. When you checked, your opponent bet again, applying pressure and trying to maximize their winnings with their made hand.

wrongReasons: Q 10 has similar strength, but look at when the aggression shifted — the turn card connects with the correct hand in a way it doesn't connect with Q 10.
J 5 makes a two pair here — a hand that strong would have raised earlier and pushed harder for maximum value. The measured sizing in this hand points to something weaker.
Q 7 doesn't benefit from the turn card the way the correct hand does. A player holding only high card wouldn't have the confidence to raise on that street.
hand.explanation:
Answer: 8 K

Your opponent's hand fits the betting pattern because they had high card on the flop but didn't have a strong hand to bet with. The King on the turn improved their hand to One Pair, which is when they started playing more aggressively, and they maintained this aggression on the river with their One Pair.
